item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images that were used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the first image is owned by sprol.com (as indicated here), and the second image could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are hinted to be some tulpa-like aspects to this, but the main component is a misanthropic, hyper-patriotic ghost. (The tulpa aspects being that they did something to exaggerate or fabricate these traits.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oooh, is this a tulpa of some sort? :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Man, I would *hate* to see this thing break containment today. Edward Snowden, Crimea, Syria…it'd be a disaster. The Foundation better pray that Faraday Cage is impenetrable.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had an annoying little brother who used to say, "horror movies with ghosts in them are stupid. Ghosts can't hurt you. Why would anyone be scared of ghosts?"

This. This is why you're scared of ghosts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You're right that the deterrent effect is dependent on the enemy having knowledge of it. However, that doesn't mean that the public needs to know about its existence. As long as you've got a deterrent in place that the other nations' government officials know about, then it serves its purpose.

In the case of the real-life Dead Hand, its existence wasn't really made public during the Cold War, and even now the information put out by the Russian government is contradictory and vague. You can bet, though, that NATO's nuclear planners knew that the Soviet Union possessed a fail-deadly second strike mechanism, and that's really who needs to know for such things.

In the article, the Soviets clued the Americans in pretty early about the existence of Dead Hand. It was meant to convey that there would be no doubt whatsoever that a second strike would be ordered, because the decision to activate the system was now out of human hands. This is conveyed by the fact that the Soviets and the Americans approach the Foundation together for help in the article; the Soviets have no wish to actually end the world, and don't want NATO to interpret any weird signal traffic as an impending attack, so they brought Western intelligence into the loop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is based on a real thing. The point of this is that what the public thinks the Dead Hand is and what the Dead Hand really is are totally different. Or part of the point, anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1, because I liked concept.

But unfortunately there is one problem with it. Deterrent makes sense only if everyone know about it. What Soviets wanted to do in case of successful project? Announce to entire world "Hey, we have now ghost that will launch nukes even if all Ruskies are dead, PHEAR US"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I edited the page back to revision 7 because the image wasn't working properly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm already a sucker for anything related to the Soviets. But an experiment gone horribly awry who insists on taking the whole world down with the Union?
If only I could +100

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded: Self-upvoting with staff permission to counteract confirmed case of malicious downvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
STUR WORS
… I posted that AFTER it was made. I'm such a moron.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like that part, too. I was thinking maybe it'd be something like a network or something connecting to the launch controls.

I was actually rather disappointed that it all came down to "Ghooooosts! OOOOOH SPOOKY! ONE IS A PSYCHOPATH WHOOOO!" in the end. Lots of people like it, but I feel rather failed in the end.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The US finally weaponizes 789-J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article has two things that hit very specific sweet-spots for me. First, I love SCPs that make ordinary things (nuclear weapons are fairly ordinary, by Foundation standards) incredibly dangerous. Second, I love the whole era of the Cold War. Sure, it was a terribly dangerous period, but it's an era that interests me intensely. I salute you, good sir, for this. Glorious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Butt fart

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
NECRO

I am actually very seriously working on this thing's US equivalent.

I will give you a small hint, the name has two words, both four letters long each, just like Dead Hand.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What hits me about this is how much "real world" potential it has to have been attempted. The reading I have done on the soviet cold war era Psychic experimentation makes me see this as a very plausible scenario for being funded. Combined with what was really implemented in the M.A.D. plans, well, perhaps this is not so far fetched as some would like to think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is excellent! It actually gave me a nightmare.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It occurs to me that both of the pictures are stretched beyond the actual image's size. It might be worthwhile to shrink these so there aren't any artifacts, or to find a larger version of each.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice Reagan, almost got every human being killed lol. Anyway, upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enough information here to develop a game around this. It is exceptionally rare to encounter an SCP this historically accurate and yet engaging. Of course this is worthy of an upvote (not that you need anymore) +1

item.title: Re: 100+
item.content:encoded: Thanks for your part in contributing to that success. I'm really happy with how it turned out.
I'm glad this was a success.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well-researched, well-thought out, and a legitimately terrifying picture, to boot!

Upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I'm happy to see this made it onto the Russian site!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great. You somehow managed a Russian SCP that actually looks plausible!
Your upvotes are well-deserved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just freaking amazing. Love the images and the idea. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is lovely. I mean - a SCP based on real world events, plus we all know how crazy some of the russian researches were, so they conjured a ghost entity.. man, I like this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really, really, really good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, I'm with Scan on this one. Amazing SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is absolutely fantastic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh dear god. New favorite SCP. Good work dude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was digging this as soon it was revealed that the nuclear launching device was a ghost, which is incredibly silly but works somehow, but the logs really sold me on it. Great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 15 members on site. The way I'd envisioned it, there would always be a small group on duty at any given time ready to respond to emergencies, but crafting the reports could be done in such a way that the reports could all be assembled on a typical 9-5 schedule and disseminated throughout the day (they would of course build up several weeks of programming so as to be a couple of weeks ahead at any given time).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At all times, the Information Control Team shall be staffed with no fewer than fifteen (15) members and housed in Dormitory 3-F, adjacent to Studio 1-A.

This seems a bit ambiguous to me. At least 15 members which are to be awake and on duty at any one time, or 15 members who are always on site, though not all of them will be awake at the same time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some of the implications on how this thing was made really struck home for me. Long, but an excellent piece.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent, excellent Keter article. I always hold them to a higher standard, and this one works very well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am the ghoooost nuuuuke, ooooooooh!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh holy mother of god…

NEVER tell this thing that the Cold War is over. NEVER!

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1st and 2nd point: Dude it's the ultimate revenge weapon, you literally can't kill him and he will use everything at his disposal to kill you!
3rd point: Where did you get the idea that it could comprehend advanced ideas? It almost seems like an attack dog's kind of intelligence to me, trained to do one specific thing (shoot nukes) and trained to stand down when it's masters soothe it.
4th point: ICBM's cost a lot of goddamn money, they're what bankrupted the Soviets after all, and it costs almost as much to safely decommission the bastards! Any option to keep a hold of a model of working, functional ICBM's rather than replace them would be preferable to the Russians.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though I'm not much for history, I like this, and you've got another upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The first ghost gives me a mental image of a mad scientist looking over his failed creation.

"No, what have I done? You were meant to rise up against your creator and destroy us all! But you are far too beautiful and full of compassion. Where have I gone wrong?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: walk softly, and carry an anomalous stick.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I'm going to write an SCP that ghost-built the Panama Canal. The Foundation has to provide it with a hill to charge up every few hours, or else it annoys staff by saying "bully for you sir" over and over again. I think this will work out well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That second picture looks kinda like teddy roosevelt. This is now my headcanon and there is nothing you can do to stop me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait…1984…posted June 25th…nuclear war…

You son of a bitch. I wish I could upvote just for that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dr. Handlove, or How I Learned to Stop Worrying and Love the Ghost

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well-written, a fascinating backdrop and a good concept. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually really like this. Also, where the hell did you find the second picture?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. I really like the concept, as the Cold War was one era of history I could definitely see both sides creating SCPs as weapons, or in this case, failsafes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well written but it doesn't really grab me. I might just not be very interested in the subject matter. Neutral vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Peace Walker was designed to be the ultimate nuclear deterrent.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good and you should feel good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted hard. This hit all my sweet spots.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nice. Makes me wonder what the US has up its sleeve…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. Not sure how to address that one.

2. The intent was to address Dead Hand's weakness; it still needed to be activated by a living, breathing human. Many people doubted its existence for a long time because the idea of a fully automated doomsday machine, even one that needed to be switched "on" by a person during a crisis, sounds insane. And that was my thought; how effective a deterrent is it if there's any doubt that you'll actually put it online? This thing is already dead, and full of unending malice. It's not human anymore, and thus can be a reliable threat in a way a person never could be. Whether that works, I can't say.

And frankly, the "one installation at a time thing" isn't going to matter that much since it has all the time it needs to start launching; it's not going to die in any aftereffects of a nuclear exchange. This, of course, depends on the supposition that the Russians have done a good job keeping enemy intelligence on all of their silos to a minimum.

3. In my vision of this thing's intelligence, it has a warped "intelligence"; it can't communicate or think of anything other than a set (albeit complicated) of parameters specified by its creators; think of it as an extremely complicated computer algorithm, stuck in time in the early 80s (this, btw, is one of its design flaws; this project was a failure, which necessitated the Soviets revealing it to the Foundation and asking for help). Foundation staff would be afraid to tell it that the Soviet Union no longer exists, since its designers hadn't accounted for this scenario except in the case of "Soviet Union no longer exists due to nuclear holocaust". Which of course would set it off.

4. Someone with more technical knowledge than myself can correct me here, but I think that it's really, REALLY difficult to completely overhaul your ICBM technology and redeploy. The Russians would not want to scrap the backbone of their nuclear arsenal if they could help it (after all, they still need to maintain a deterrent to this day), and they have faith in the Foundation to keep this thing contained.

I hope this provides a little more context, and thanks for taking the time to comment!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really want to upvote this, as I feel the idea has a lot of potential, but some things bother me:

First of all, making it humanoid. In and of itself this is not a deal breaker, but it feels kind of out of place. When it's revealed to be a ghost, though, that kind of puts it off for me.

Second of all, how is it supposed to be an effective nuclear deterrent if its speed is so limited and it can only launch nukes from one facility at a time? The real Dead Hand, which could launch all of Russia's nukes at once, seems way more efficient.

Third, this thing can obviously comprehend advanced ideas, so has anyone tried to explain to it that the Cold War is over and the USSR no longer exists?

Fourth, if it only effects that one type of nuclear missile, couldn't it be neutralized by simply decommissioning all of those missiles (and, if necessary, transferring their payloads into a different type of missile?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: New SCP going up, "Dead Hand". All critiques/suggestions welcome.
